	DST Viability		
Answer the question: Can we detect RIF and INH in tongue swabs?

Big picture: can we offer RIF/INH detection as a product feature?
It will likely extend our timeline and the regulatory process will be painful, but it is a great differentiator from other products.

Goal:
Get 50 samples from R2D2 (or another pre-approved sample source), all TB positive. 25 should be RIF mono-resistant and 25 should be INH mono-resistant. Test them with the V2 device and determine sensitivity for the RIF and INH assays.

Full credit for testing all 50 samples, regardless of the results.

When this goal is complete, get Jeremy's approval for a new goal to do the following:
Test spiked swabs with known concentrations of RIF- and INH-resistant TB in a BSL-3 lab at UNPAD, UGM, or another potential clinical trial site with the ability to culture TB strains.

Regulatory Status	Pre-clinical	Global Fund: In July 2025, the MiniDock MTB Test was added to the Global Fund procurement catalog with an Expert Review Panel for Diagnostics (ERPD) listing. This allows it to be used in programs supported by the Global Fund.

Indonesia: In August 2024, Pluslife received an import medical device license from the Indonesian government for its tuberculosis nucleic acid test card. This was the first certification in Indonesia for a dual-sample TB detection technology (sputum and tongue swab).

European Union (EU): The device has CE certification and is approved for sale in the EU.

World Health Organization (WHO): Pluslife's diagnostic solutions are currently advancing toward WHO prequalification. They will likely recieve PQ prior to the November 2025 Union Conference.

USA and Canada: MiniDock is not approved for sale in either country following a recall of Pluslife's COVID-19 tests and restrictions by FDA.	WHO: Approved for sputum samples only. They will likely receive PQ prior to the November 2025 Union Conference.

Indonesia: Approved for sputum only. Integrated into the National TB Programme diagnostic algorithm.

FDA: Approved Xpert MTB/RIF for sputum only. Xpert Ultra has not received FDA approval for any sample type.

EU: The device has CE certification and is approved for sale in the EU for sputum only.	WHO: The Truenat MTB Plus test was endorsed by the WHO in 2020 for the diagnosis of TB using sputum samples.

The Truenat MTB ULTIMA has not yet received a formal WHO endorsement for any sample type. 

Indonesia: Approved for sputum only. Not widely implemented.	FDA: CRISPR-TB blood test received Breakthrough Device Designation* in January 2025. The saliva test has not been approved, and is still in the R&D phase.

*BDD doesn't comment on the safety or efficacy of the product. It simply provides an accelerated regulatory pathway for novel devices that diagnose or treat life-threatening diseases.	"Investigational prototype" as of August 2025.	No regulatory status. Undergoing clinical trials in China. Some funding from Gates Foundation.

=== Izin Edar Checklist ===
Izin Edar Checklist for a Domestic In Vitro Diagnostic (IVD) Test in Indonesia
Phase	Requirement	Document/Action	Status	Notes
1: Pre-Submission Preparation
	Company & Authorization
	Legal Business Entity	- NIB (Nomor Induk Berusaha) from the OSS system	FALSE	Your company must be registered as a legal entity in Indonesia with the appropriate business classification code (KBLI).	32509
		- Production Certificate (Sertifikat Produksi) for medical devices from the MoH.	FALSE	Mandatory for all domestic manufacturers. Ensure this is up to date.
	Product Information
	Risk Classification	- A clear statement of the intended use of the device.	FALSE	The MoH will use this to determine if your device is Class C or D. As a PCR test for TB, it will likely be Class D due to the high public health risk.
	Quality Management System	- CPAKB (Cara Produksi Alat Kesehatan yang Baik) certification from the MoH.	FALSE	This is the local equivalent of GMP (Good Manufacturing Practice) for medical devices.
		- OR a certified ISO 13485 (if applicable).	FALSE
2: Dossier Preparation (ASEAN CSDT)
	Part 1: Executive Summary	- Overview of the device, its intended use, principle of operation, and a summary of the technical documentation.	FALSE	Should be a concise summary of the entire dossier.
	Part 2: Device Description	- Detailed product specification, including all components and reagents.	FALSE	Include Material Safety Data Sheets (MSDS) for all reagents and chemicals used.
		- Intended use, indications, contraindications, and warnings.	FALSE	Must be consistent across all documents.
		- Labels, packaging, and Instructions for Use (IFU) in Bahasa Indonesia.	FALSE	The IFU must be comprehensive and easily understood.
	Part 3: Design & Manufacturing Info	- A description of the manufacturing process and facility.	FALSE	Link to your CPAKB certification.
		- Summary of design verification and validation reports.	FALSE	This includes stability studies, performance data, and other test results.
	Part 4: Risk Management	- Comprehensive risk analysis report based on ISO 14971.	FALSE	This document is especially critical for a high-risk IVD like a TB test.
		- Risk management file, including risk mitigation measures.	FALSE
	Part 5: Clinical Evidence	- Clinical Evaluation Report (CER) from the manufacturer.	FALSE	For Class C/D devices, this must include strong clinical data.
		- Local clinical data from a national reference lab or a reputable healthcare facility in Indonesia.	FALSE	Often required for higher-risk devices to demonstrate performance in the local population.
	Part 6: Special Considerations	- TKDN (Tingkat Komponen Dalam Negeri) Certificate.	FALSE	Not mandatory for Izin Edar, but essential if you want to participate in government procurement.
		- Biocompatibility report for any materials in direct contact with the patient (e.g., the tongue swab itself).	FALSE
3: Online Submission & Approval
	Online Submission	- Upload the complete dossier to the MoH portal (Regalkes).	FALSE	Ensure all files are in the correct format (e.g., PDF) and named according to the system's requirements.
	Fee Payment	- Await an invoice from the MoH after initial submission.	FALSE	Payment must be made within the specified timeframe (typically 7 calendar days).
	Review & Evaluation	- Respond to any inquiries or requests for additional data from the MoH evaluators.	FALSE	Be prepared for a detailed review process that can take up to 80 working days for a Class D device.
	Issuance	- Receive the official Nomor Izin Edar (NIE) from the MoH.	FALSE	The license will be valid for 5 years.
4: Post-Market & Maintenance
	Post-Market Surveillance	- Establish a system for monitoring product performance and collecting feedback from the market.	FALSE	Report any serious adverse events to the MoH.
	License Renewal	- Prepare the renewal application before the expiration date.	FALSE	The process is similar to the new application but may be more streamlined if no major changes have been made.
	Change Notification	- Notify the MoH of any changes to the product, manufacturing process, or company details.	FALSE

Sonication sample swap sample (water to resus) problem reconfirm and RCA PoC


A .Background


Based on what happen on V2 instrument at a few months ago, we found 


“ after stress test,  transducer (PZTOD35-32SO) could not adapt the soften after-heating vial condition which sign by bad cavitation or need a few cycles attempt first to produce the stable cavitation “ 


That condition give assumption  : 


            1. The transducer getting weak  
            2. The transducer more stable (state as “after burn-in transducer”)
 
For number 1 assumption we have consequences like : 
            1. The transducer getting a highly lifetime degradation
            2. Need to determine the lifecycle level for transducer to put on current instrument to determine sample count capability for handling the risk


For number 2 assumption we have consequences like :
            1. The stable transducer should be handled by a module whose mean form application side we need to determine the more properly and fit to purpose for application which is conveyed by mechanical module. 
            2. Need to state the burn-in method in the bench (the burn-in process stated by experts like Hussein Shekani). 


B. Direction


From both assumptions , for number one assumption I think it seems not to be close to the death transducer because the transducer is still active but it is sensitive to misalignment of horn-vials. If I assume the new transducer has an amplitude spike and that condition could give an additional push when sonicate and compromise the misalignments the assumption number 2 is more close. Because at that time the transducer seems active but it has less amplitude and low anomaly of  spike probability. 


As additional to strengthen the reason, until this time we don't state any burn-in protocol on our sonicator production at bench. So I think the number 2 assumption is closer. Morely, in normal use , we don't find any death transducer even there is transducer which use by Amry to do some bench test which high probability of harmful , and the Transducer doesn't death until now. 


C. PoC of Number 2 Assumption


I did some testing with ultrasonic mic which have a high probability of harmfulness like : 
            1. Sonication on no vial (give Vrms > 1kV)
            2. Sonication on dry vial 
            3. Sonication on horn-vial tilt 


After that test we found that The PZTOD25-47SOB#1 cannot sonicate the resuspension buffer if I put a shim at the back to make tilt condition but in opposite if I use water it could sonicate

So based on that test I think we got  : 


            1. We prove the self aligning transducer’s horn to vial could handle the resuspension after the transducer got a harmful test. 
            2. We need to redefine what is important for enhance versatility for sample condition when sonication , is it the Electrical Q, mechanical frequency cutoff or the well-distributed coupling force for vial-horn 


    D. What next direction


   I know this partial test need to be compared and strengthened by this several test  
            1. To prove the burn-in state and non burn-in state , we need also test the new transducer as comparator (this test hold because board and oscilloscope have a tour to amry’s trafo feedback test) 
            2. Doing more test on stability of Vrms (especially for resuspension) and temperature in vial  increase, to see the the alignment effect to Vrms which represent the accoustical & electrical impedance
            3. Starting to state the burn-in protocol for preparing sonication system

Sonicator team  on “Bandwidth” redefinition 
A.Backgorund
The bandwidth word  which we state  like at this comments thread , is no matter on the statement after Amry invented his DIY impedance analyzer by constant current source. The weird thing which we feel after measurement is 
 “ The Impedance measurement always state very narrow bandwidth (couple ten or hertz or even under) but in real physical system The frequency could shift up to 2khz based cavitation existing “ 
The DIY impedance measurement also state the anti resonance which feeling different with Transducer Physical anti resonance
So we think the Sonicator team Core Lead should make a clear on this situation to avoid misunderstanding on both EE & ME sides to avoid misdirection and causing wasted resources.


B.Bandwidth definition from EE and Common sound-acoustical field
In simply way, 
  

So -3dB as criterion for anti resonance when shift little by little from resonance frequency would note as antiresonance frequency And resulting bandwidth
C.The antiresonance behavior based on mechanical vibration
The condition is like the picture below, so mechanical said on little-off-resonance the displacement Dy start happen but its very small, and still state as proper-longitudinal vibration. So we state before on this Antiresonance frequency minus Resonant frequency as bandwith, Which in the physical field we found when we sweep any frequency around resonance frequency we found an area  of no cavitation in both negative and positive side.  
  



D. Why the electrical cannot well-detected mechanical bandwidth verison
 Its because the EE bandwidth detect on electrical dB which represent energy from currents amps flow source input which is come from one dimensional displacement from piezo electric
  

  

 The EE bandwidth which state by Amry’s DIY impedance analyzer State on only pure longitudinal which should have a quick response because the fully amplitude just only on pure longitudinal and one dimensional displacement which effected converted as electrical db of energy and opposite, when the transducer get transversal is cause the energy which use for Displacement Dy , cannot detected by piezo because the one dimensional typical works of piezo. That's why the energy drop from Amry’s DIY impedance analyzer exceeds 3dB if shift with only a little couple of ten hertz.
 In other words, the tools only detect the very pure longitudinal component of the mechanical vibration mode, a condition that almost never occurs in real transducers because structural stress equilibrium naturally introduces transversal and radial components.
E. The Redefining 
 The mechanical bandwidth re-state as 
“ Mechanical Frequency Cutoff “ 
 Meanwhile the Electrical bandwidth still has the same terms to make EE not confused for doing capacitor and electrical Q research etc.

Ultrasonic Mic Update

d33 Measurement Tools


  



The determination of d33 piezo, we need a charge amplifier equation:
  

  

And this equation, substitute both of equation and we have the d33 formulation:
  



________________


The procedures for this action are below:
            1. Prepare the sample
            * Mount the piezo disk/stack between two flat electrodes/clamps. Ensure good electrical contact and uniform mechanical contact.
            * If using a large external capacitor (for short-circuit condition), connect it across the piezo terminals (parallel) before measurement.
            * Zero out the charge amplifier (i.e. reset to 0 V output with no load) to nullify offsets.
            2. Apply known force
            * Using the weight scale for the big force to apply a controlled compressive force (in Newtons) to the piezo. The force can be generated by a press tool or something that generates a big force. Wait until force stabilizes (no drift), ensure no mechanical tilt or misalignment.
            3. Measure the output voltage
            * Read voltage output VoutV_{out}Vout​ from the charge amplifier (in Volts or mV).
            * Record the value. If possible, repeat multiple times for accuracy / noise check.
            4. Compute d₃₃ using formula above
            5. Repeat for different force levels
            * To check linearity, repeat measurement for several force levels (e.g. small → medium → higher force) and compute d₃₃ each time.
            * Verify that d₃₃ remains roughly constant (linear behavior) across the force range. If results diverge, note non-linearity or possible mechanical issues.

            * After getting bad results from the pin heater, we moved on with the next plan which is the cap heater.
            * We set the cap heater temperature to 140°C. This is the optimum temperature to gain quick ramp up, but is still safe for the loadcell (loadcell recommended working temperature range is <40°C).

            * We then changed the way we control the bang bang. Now, instead of controlling the bang bang with pressure feedback, we control it manually with time delay. Here’s the setup based on our temperature-pressure measurements.

[dd]Oh maybe I didn't understand the table.  It looks like based on the table, the 0.3mm flexure itself is so stiff that it is providing a lot of the pre-load force.  That is not what we want.  We want the pre-load force to come from the compression spring, and the flexure just to help guide the sonicator.  So the flexure doesn't need to withstand the 900g or 2000g of force.  That is why the 0.2mm flexure might be better.  And we want it to be wide like 10mm so it is stiff in the other directions.  If we stack two 0.1mm flexures it isn't the same as one 0.2mm thick flexure because of the stiffness is t^3 in the flexure direction.  So the two 0.1mm flexures will have 4x lower stiffness than one 0.2mm flexure in the flex direction, but similar stiffness in the other directions.

Just to clarify, even in optimal conditions, fTaq with Evagreen will likely not have a later Cq than fTaq liquid in the presence of target. Evagreen will detect all amplicons, so the same amplicons as the probe, even in optimal conditions. In suboptimal conditions, since Evagreen detects all amplicons, both specific and non-specific amplicons will be detected. In order to optimize for late Cqs, you could do that with a NTC sample.
[dy]One other note. Discussion Topics:
1. Make sure to use low copy target conditions more frequently in testing, especially in Evagreen experiments to evaluate non-specific amplification (Example) If probe is included it’s helpful to show no EvaGreen in the same experiment.
2. rpoB assay sensitivity baselining, revisit primer options, etc.
3. Sequence optimization for rpoB assay detection


Previous meeting topics:
PCR enhancers
March 10 - 16,  2026
1. Continued testing of RIF melt probes and oligos. (FUSA Testing)
* WT445-450 probe does not work well with 445 SNPs (Experiments 1, 2)
* WT430-435 probe yields good asymmetric amplification and melt curves with 2-step PCR if a lower temp (58C) anneal/extend step is used (Experiment 4). 


2. Testing LNA-KatGM and LNA2-fabG1M probes for specific detection of most common ISO SNPs. (FUSA Testing)
* LNA-KatGM probe performed well (curve for SNP and no curve for WT), but LNA2-fabG1M probe yielded a low amplitude curve for WT using a 60C anneal/extend temp (Experiment 5).


3. Device Testing
* Preheat test
After we found that a temperature of 100°C gave good results, in this test we tried gradually reducing the gblocks from 10K to 100 copies. The results using a preheat temperature of 100°C can detect up to 100 copies of gblocks.
* Rif assay test
From the previous test FS2/RS2 showed good results, so in this test we want to see when using lower copies of gblocks. We used from 66k to 6.6 copies of WT gblocks and the results showed that FS2/RS2 with LNA P3 can detect up to 66 copies, meanwhile using 6.6 copies showed flat in all runs.


4. Asymmetric primers
We tried to replicate an asymmetric primer test based on FUSA’s test using FS2/R primer and Taqman probe (WT450 ROX) with WT450 and SNP450 targets (10^5 cp/ul). In the first trial, we failed to show good results because the melt curve setup for the ROX filter was unchecked. The second trial, the melt curve appeared but they did not seem as smooth as FUSA’s results. And according to Sean’s comments, we just knew that the setup for the melt curve itself needed a bit of adjustment. We then tried it again, and succeeded in demonstrating melt curve analysis using asymmetric primer in the third trial. 


5. Combination of Evagreen for Melt Curve in FAM and Asymmetric Primer for Melt Curve in ROX
For the purpose of analyzing the co-reaction between EvaGreen and the asymmetric primer, we conducted a test using the setups listed below:
1. IS6110 (FAM) + WT-450 asymmetric (ROX) + EvaGreen
2. IS6110 (FAM) + EvaGreen
3. WT-450 asymmetric (ROX)
4. IS6110 (FAM) + WT-450 + EvaGreen
In this experiment, the results indicated that the combination of EvaGreen and the asymmetric primer did not produce any negative effects on the melt curve. Instead, this combination increased the derivative reporter signal.


6. Low copies LNA 450 M
Check melt curve with low copies of SNP450 gBlock, we conducted a test using the setup listed below:
1. FS2/R 1K copies with and without TS
2. FS2/R 100 copies with and without TS
3.  FS2/Rs1a 1K copies with and without TS
4. FS2/Rs1a 100  copies with and without TS


From the result we still can’t conclude because we don’t have control that have higher concentration to check the TM.

March 03-09,  2026
1. Testing non-specific amplification with rpoB and IS6110 primer sets with fTaq and NEB hotstart Taq. (FUSA Testing).
* Non-specific amplification occurs at a higher level with tongue swab samples, even with NEB hotstart Taq (Experiments 3, 4, 5 ).


2. Testing RIF melt probes and oligos. (FUSA Testing).
* Testing unlabeled WT and SNP oligos that can be used for melt analysis characterization on the tongue swab device (Experiments 2, 5 ).
* Testing Taqman probes using asymmetric PCR and melt analysis with fTaq and HS Taq (Experiments 1, 4, 6 ).


3. Dry Chip Development
* QC dry chip batch 672
The curve quality is quite good; however, contamination of IS6110 amplicons was detected in the Channel 0. Result


* Rearrange the room layout (Dry Chip Production Lab)
The activity was initially scheduled to be carried out on Friday, March 6, 2026. However, on the same day, Tian canceled the relayout process because he felt that there was no full agreement yet. He was still questioning whether it was necessary to carry out the action. We then held another discussion on Monday, March 9, 2026, and the relayout will be carried out on Tuesday, March 10, 2026.


* Stability Test Plan
Last week, Ode searched approaches for accelerating the stability testing of our chip and found that the Arrhenius equation is commonly used for accelerated stability studies. Then he discussed this with Sean, and for now we agreed to start with a simpler approach by incubating the dry chips at higher storage temperatures and monitoring the effect on PCR performance. We’ll also check the samples at different time points, such as every three days, to observe any changes over time. We can do a quantitative Arrhenius-based analysis in the future.


4. Device Testing
* Preheat Test
After Bowo created a new sequence with several preheat temperatures, the preheat temperatures that we use : 
* 100°C
* 95°C
* 90°C
* 85°C
* 80°C
So we tested it with dry chips. We used 10K copies of IS6110 and Human gBlock. The result shows that the high temperatures show good amplification and in this test ch 0 has the highest amplification compared to the others.




* From RIF testing on the QS6, we wanna see the results as well in the device by injection method. 
                So far, we’ve got 4 best combination primers for LNA-P450m:
                1. FS1/RP
                2. FS1/RS2
                3. FS2/R
                4. FS2/RS1a
From those assays, we compared only 2 combinations (#3 and #4) with 2 combination primers for LNA-P3, because FS1 primer stock is running low. Below are the combinations that we used in device testing:
1. RpOB FP/RP - LNA P3
                2. FS2/RS2 - LNA P3 (supposed to be FS1/RS1, the primer for prev DAT testing)
                3. FS2/R - 450M
                4. FS2/RS1a - 450M
                5. buffer
Based on the results, ch2, ch3 and ch4 had similar CT values. On the other hand as we could see from the a value, ch1 (FS2/RS2 - LNA P3) gave higher signals compared to others.




5. Rif LNA-P450 Assays Testing
There are several tests that we did to choose the selected primer for LNA-P450m. As we mentioned below:
1. Primer Selection (Scientist development)
2. Tongue Swab Reactivity (Scientist development)
3. Primer Asymmetry and 4x Probe’s concentration (Scientist development)
        In the first test, we evaluated the quality of each primer and identified FS2/R and FS1/RP as the most robust and compatible primer sets for the LNA-P450m target (Figure 1). In the second test, the selected primers were evaluated under tongue swab conditions. Among the four primer combinations tested, FS2/R and F1/RP showed the least interference from the tongue swab condition and exhibited low primer–dimer formation (Figure 2&3). The final test was conducted to determine the effect of asymmetric primers (1 forward primer to 10 reverse primers) and an increased probe concentration (4× higher than the normal concentration) on PCR activity. In this test, we found that neither asymmetric primers nor a higher probe concentration produced a significant improvement in PCR performance (Figure 4).


  

Figure 1. Primer Selection Test’s Result
	  

Figure 2. Primer sets with Tongue Swab Condition
	  

Figure 3. Melt Curve of Primer Sets in TS Condition
	  

Figure 4. Primer Sets comparison from normal condition, Asymmetry primers, and higher probe
	

6. HS fTaq Tests for QS6
Previously in DE, there was several tests to create a HS fTaq with this antibody from DSBio. There was no significant improvement in the HS version and the tests was not resumed. A quick look at the older journals shows that the fTaq was not mixed 1:1: with the antitaq, which could’ve resulted in just some of the fTaq in the mix to become HS, with most of it being just plain old taq with the same performance. 
Now there is more interest in testing out a HS version of fTaq, and here I tested it out with and without TS samples to see if this helps in overcoming the inhibitory effect of TS samples, and increasing sensitivity by reducing non specific amplicons. The HS fTaq performed much better than the regular version, however, the amount of  amplicons on the lower Tm side remained somewhat significant when compared to the non HS version. It is also interesting to note that all of our melt curves contain much more background compared to tests done during DE days (comparison only relevant to no TS rxn). 
I only have less than 10uL of the antibody left, but have ordered more that should arrive by the end of March. For now, all of HS fTaq tests can only be done in liquid form as the antibody itself contains glycerol. If we want more for dried chips, dialysing the glycerol out with pulse could be an option.

* rpoB-Fs1/Rs1 primer dimer formation may be more significant in tongue swab samples (Experiment 3).


2. Lung hospital testing
We’ve tested 60 samples at the lung hospital and conclude that 46/60 overall results match with the GeneXpert (excluding 5 sample aborts). Two samples indicated RIF resistance, yet we still couldn't guess it based on our amplification curves. We probably want to check the LNA 450M first, as the other options for RIF assays this week.


3. Dry Chip Development


* QC dry chip batch 670
The curve quality is quite good; however, contamination of IS6110 amplicons was detected in the Channel 0  and 3. and contamination of Human amplicons / DNA was detected in the Channel 3. result                 
* QC dry chip batch 671
The curve quality is good, and the negative control is better than before; however, there is still a very small amount of WT contamination. We will begin relocating the QC area outside the production lab curtain. result


* Contamination Issue
After a discussion with Bayu (ME Consumables), Munis (ME Consumables), Ode, and Funky (Production Support), several factors were identified as potential causes of contamination:
1. Silicon handling
2. Chip handling
3. Vial handling
4. Cap handling
5. Cross-contamination from the general laboratory during the transfer of materials or reagents
6. Amplicon contamination during QC
Follow-up actions to be taken:
1. Perform UV treatment at a specified intensity on the consumables used.
2. Rearrange the room layout. The QC Dry Chip area and the production area will be physically separated by curtain. The new layout has been discussed, and Funky is currently preparing the electrical setup. The relayout is planned to be carried out this Friday.
4.  fTaq vs Meridian with IS assay.
Here I compared the performance of fTaq vs Meridian with the IS 6 and 10 assays, with and without human assay and TS. And it is pretty clear that the weak IS assay performance that have been observed is worse with fTaq compared to meridian.  Note : the meridian mastermix (polymerase +assay+gblock) was prepared the day before the test ( leftovers from this experiment) and was stored in 4C overnight. 
5. Preliminary test with Proteinase K
I have been considering trying out PK as a way to reduce the strong inhibitory effect that we observe with saliva for some time, especially with fTaq. PK has been reported in multiple studies to help with sensitivity in PCR with saliva (mostly with RNA extraction for covid detection). There was a concern on if the PK would inhibit the fTaq if we don’t wash it away / remove it from the mixture, but a heat precipitation step was thought to be enough to remove any negative effects. However from these results, the addition of PK has slightly worse results when compared to no PK.  
* The stock of PK that we have is from 2023, and has been stored in our -20C freezer all this time.
* 0.25 ug of PK (following this paper)  was added to the TS and incubated at 58C for 1 hr. We could change the amount and time used if necessary.

February 10 - 16,  2026
1. Device Testing
* Assay Compatibility Testing
We checked the compatibility of LNA P 3 ROX with KAT G and FAB G using DsBio in all reactions and we did it with injection set up. The results show that KAT G and FAB G have good performance. The graph is high, meanwhile the LNA P 3 has lower amplification not as high as KAT G and FAB G.


* Preliminary Test
We also did a preliminary test in devices 003, 005, and 006 before going into the BSL testing. We did it with dry chips and we also added 1M copies/600 uL of IS6110, WT and Human gBlock on the resuspension buffer. The results showed that all the channels have amplification without the tongue swab samples and on the FAM channel the stdev value shows a number below 1, except on the ROX channel.


* Preliminary test in BSl-2 inhouse
The previous reproducibility of 4 devices (without TS sample) were good by its stdev less than 1. Otherwise, inconsistent results were observed across the devices when TS sample was introduced (esp on low copies gblock, its start to on off at 10K cp/gblock). We suspected whether it's due to the inhibitory sample itself or the sonicator process decreasing low copies gblock performance. We want to test low copies of gblock on devices just to see their performance without TS sample. since the previous reproducibility results used high copies gblock


To find out the sonication effect on low copies of gblock, we did test using QS6 using several concentrations of IS6110 gblock (100k - 100 cp) without TS sample. Then the results, the effect of sonication on high copies gblock showed significant results on the cqs and the curve amplitude, while in lower gblocks (1000 - 100) only effecting on the curves amplitudes. These results werent as our assumption because we think that after the sonication process, the gblock will decrease in performance. One of our suspects was, we mixed the fTaq at the very first with the samples then kept them in the fridge before the PCR process. Maybe good results came from non specific amplicons. and the other suspect maybe, sonication process mixed the gblock very well eventho we did manual tip mix to aliquot the sample as before sonication.
We then conducted the 2nd exp exactly the same as the 1st exp to validate the results. In this test we added fTaq right after all the samples were ready to be run in QS6. Therefore, the same result pattern was observed as the previous test. as the conclusions, there is no impact before and after sonication on low cp gblock. The prelim results in inhouse BSL with 4 devices were bad on low cp gblock was purely because of the introducing of TS sample as the inhibitory things




2. Dry Chip Development


* Selection of the rifampicin assay
Based on the results and discussions with the team, we will use only one rifampicin assay so that Ftaq can still be tested on the same chip with DS Bio HS at RSPAW. We selected between the LNAP-3 FS1/RS1 and LNAP-3 FS1/RS1a assays, as both showed better performance compared to the other LNAP-3 assays.
Our selection was based on run results from batch 665, which was tested with TS samples, as well as batches 660, 661, and 662, which were run without TS samples. The results showed that LNAP-3 FS1/RS1 was relatively more consistent than LNAP 3 RS1 Fs1 a. Therefore, we selected the LNAP 3 RS1 Fs1 assay to be used on the chip that will be tested at RSPAW.
* QC Batch 666

* QC Batch 667
The curve quality is quite good. DS Bio IS6110 and IS1081, DS Bio Rif FS1 RS1, DS Bio Kat G, and Ftaq FS1 RS1 have A values of 200–250. DS Bio HS Human, as usual with this sequence, has an A value below 100. Unfortunately, Ftaq Kat G has an A value below 200. We also still detected human DNA contamination and slight WT contamination in Kat G, even though general cleaning has been performed. We suspect there is contamination in the Kat G assay. We will try replacing it with a new stock. result

* Reproducibilty
We check the reproducibility of the new sequence in all V2 devices. We run it with injection, vial, and full sequence.
* Run #1 - #8 used injected sequences (IS6110 6600 copies and Human 6600 copies).
* Run #9 - #12 used vial sequence with dried chip without heating and sonicating (IS6110 1M copies, MSM 1M copies and Human 1M cp).

-  We got some aborted in full sequence because the latex is leaking, so we must rework the latex first before running the process.


* Reproducibility with gBlock
The next day we continued the reproducibility test using device 003, 005, 006. In this test we used the gBlock of MSM, IS6110 , and Human with 1M copies and we used the full sequence set up. 
We still got several aborted runs because the latex leaked even after we retouch and used a latex cover. So that’s why in several runs we replaced the latex with a new one and successfully completed the run.


* Reproducibility with MSM bacteria
The next day we continued the reproducibility test, in this test we used the MSM bacteria with 100k CFU/600uL and 1M copies/600 uL of IS6110 and Human gBlock. The results when using the MSM bacteria the stdev can below than 1 between run and devices, and also in device 3 at channel 3 the ct shows around 17 in most of run it’s different from other devices that makes higher stdev than other channels.




2. Dry Chip Development
Carried out production of Dry Chips for batches 662 and 663 and performed quality control. Both batches showed good quality curves; however, there was amplicon contamination. Therefore, we conducted a general cleaning of the production laboratory on February 5, 2026.


Plan
* Test the stability of the Dry Chip at 80°C for 30 minutes after 1 month of storage. The test will be conducted on February 13, 2026.
* If the chips remain stable after one month, we will begin preparing 36 chips for a one-year stability study. We will test 3 chips per month.
* Test the tolerance limits of the baking temperature. We will test 5°C below and 5°C above the set point, so the temperatures to be tested are 75°C, 80°C, and 85°C.
* Prepare Dry Chips for in-house testing.
* Prepare Dry Chips for testing with RSPAW and R2D2 samples.




3. DSBio LOD with TS

We then retested it again after making new reagent stocks and the results showed that we still had persistent amplicons (the amplicons produced having the same or similar peak with the target (IS6110) by the melt curves) and the same pattern was observed when the addition of TS had inhibitory effects towards DSB performance as previous results. Because of those persistent amplicons, we did general lab cleaning the next day.

   3. DSBio LOD with TS sample in QS6.
Link to report : Scientist development
This was done to gauge how TS samples affect DSB in QS6. It showed that DSB had robust performance with templates as low as 5 copies, and produced target specific amplicons when judged by its meltcurves. The inhibitory effect of TS However, the NC is observed to have higher signal than expected. Current hypothesis is reagent contamination, and the addition of evagreen makes any and all amplicon to be highlighted. It is curious to observe, however, that judging by its meltcurves, the NC has a peak that’s similar to the target used (IS6110).

January 20, 2026 - MgCl2 concentrations affecting tongue swab sample curves (FUSA)
As I mentioned in the January 19 Scientist Meeting below, I have done some testing in FUSA in which I have evaluated different concentrations of MgCl2 (1.25 mM to 3.75 mM) in the PCR reaction with low copy target (100 cp/rxn) tongue swab and control samples. At mid and higher level MgCl2 concentrations, Taqman curves were more robust with early Cts, but high concentrations did result in higher ratios of non-specific primer dimer amplification. See Experiments 3 through 5 (link). It is worth evaluating MgCl2 concentrations with our tongue swab device and low target concentration. DSBio example from H2 2025 (link).

January 12-19,  2026
   1. Device Testing
   * Preliminary test, MSM with HS DSBio
Since we did not have data of MSM cells using HS DsBio, we tried to collect this data using liquid set up.
We spike 30uL to swab stick from concentrations : 
   * 5 M CFU / mL -> final concentration 6750 cfu / 40 uL.
   * 2.5 M CFU / mL -> final concentration 3375 cfu / 40uL.
   * 1.25 M CFU / mL -> final concentration 1687 cfu / 40uL.
The mechanism is we heat and sonicate the sample in the first, then we take 27uL of sample and add with 13uL of formulation of assay and polymerase, after that the solution is injected into the channel and run in the 005 device.




   * BSL-2 inhouse testing
The first objective is to test MSM cells that spiked to TS and sputum samples. In this test, 20 uL from 5 M CFU/mL MSM cells was spiked onto tongue swab (100K CFU on TS sample). Afterwards, the tongue swab is going through a full process in device 005 with 600 uL resuspension buffer. And then, est final concentration is around 40/600*100,000 = 6,666 CFU/channel. On the other hand for sputum test, 100 uL from MSM stock (16 M CFU/mL) was added to sputum 17 (saliva) which est vol around 500 uL. est final concentration of MSM after blank flock swab dipped to saliva is around 80K - 100K CFU/swab. What we observed from this test, so far: HS DSBio perform better than the ftaq to detect MSM and the curves were sigmoid while using blank flock swab (ctrl), but HS DSBio curves start sloping when we introduce TS and sputum sample. Another thing is, it is nice to see that the fTaq could detect MSM cells, because from the previous data, when we try it with liquid set up with MSM cell, ftaq couldn't detect it (the curve was almost flat, data: 12/03/2025; 12/05/2025; 12/11/2025)


The second objective is to test IS6110 gBlock 100K copies/600 uL that spiked into vial tube. Run #1 and #2, run as a control without TS or sputum, all channel were amplified on DSbio and Ftaq. Run #3 and #4, added TS sample to vial, the result shows on Ftaq has weak amplification while DSbio shows strong curve. Run #5 and 6 (contains TS samples that was dipped to sputum that was spiked with gblock) were aborted early in the cycling process. The sample used in run#5 was visibly more viscous compared to normal TS samples, which could've caused the run to be aborted since it hindered the liquid movement. In run#7 and *8 (blank floqswab dipped into saliva-purulent sputum, fTaq did not exhibit any activity and DSBio produced curve with poor quality for the IS6110 assay. Both polymerase performed well for the human assay. In run#9 and #10 (blank floqswab dipped into saliva), both polymerase performed well and matched it's control performance

Figure 1. Trend of a-value
  

The drying parameter 50C 80 min shows good stability in two weeks. The CT and b-values showed low standard deviation across both IS6110-FAM and Human-ROX channels, indicating good stability after drying (Table 1). In contrast, the a-value showed higher variability, especially at early time points. However, as shown in Table 2 and Figure 1, the average a-value decreased sharply from day 0 to day 6, then became relatively stable after that for both DSBio HS and fTaq. Although some fluctuation was still observed, the overall trend suggests that the dry chip maintained acceptable performance for up to two weeks. 


80C 30 mins
Since the previous experiment showed no significant difference in performance between chips baked at 50°C for 80 minutes and those baked at 80°C for 30 minutes, we repeated the stability test at 80°C for 30 minutes. New testing was conducted on day 0 and day 1 and will be continued for up to two weeks. The 50°C for 80 minutes parameter is still feasible for producing 36 chips per day; however, the 80°C for 30 minutes condition provides greater time flexibility, especially  for Quality Control or to enable a further increase in production quantity.

The cross reaction tests were done with several rpoB primer sets (rpoB, Fs1/Rs1, Fs1/Rs1a, Fs1/Rs2a) with IS6110 IS8110 gblocks (66k cps), with and without TS sample. January 6, 2026 - Using anneal/extend temp to improve sensitivity[a][b]
In the FUSA meeting today, we discussed a quick test that could be done to potentially reduce non-specific amplification and increase sensitivity with low target concentrations. We recommend testing increasing the anneal/extend temperature to reduce non-specific amplification (could be relevant to both fTaq and DSBio reactions). Below is an example gradient PCR experiment that I performed last month with fTaq and 100 copies of IS6110 gBlock and Evagreen. In this experiment (69C-circles and 65C-crosses) yielded more robust and target-specific curves than 61C anneal/extend. It is definitely worth revisiting increased anneal/extend temps across a concentration range[c][d][e][f][g] of target on the tongue swab device. Since previous optimization has only been performed at high target concentration, the impact on specificity and sensitivity was likely masked.

         1. Demonstrating the specificity risk of allowing an fTaq reaction that was preheated to 85C and allowed to dwell at 85C before PCR cycling. (FUSA Testing).
Preheating the reaction to 85C prior to fTaq addition, then allowing the reaction to remain at 85C for 3 min (the time needed on the current tongue swab device to cool from 85C to 60C) results in high levels of non-specific primer-dimer amplification and no target-specific amplification -link.
However, preheating the reaction to 95C prior to fTaq addition, then allowing the reaction to remain at 95C for 3 min resulted in robust Taqman curves and target-specific amplicon melt curves - link.


         2. Using touchdown PCR to improve fTaq reaction specificity and work with a condition in which the cold block on the tongue swab can cool from denature temp to anneal/extend temp during PCR. (FUSA Testing).
Preheating the reaction to 95C prior to fTaq addition, then proceeding directly to PCR in which the anneal/extend temp decreases from 95C to 65C during cycling resulted in robust target-specific amplicon curves and melt curves and very late PCR non-specific curves for the NTC - link.


         3. Preheat Test
Because when we did with the dried chips the results were not good. In this test we try with liquid set up. We have 3 parameters in here :
         * Ch 0 sample without preheat (normal condition)
         * Ch 1 sample is heated (except fTaq & Assays)
         * Ch 2 sample is heated (include fTaq & Assays)
         * Ch 3 resuspension buffer
         * Ch 4 resuspension buffer
We heated samples with conventional PCR in 85C for 3 mins. After that we injected the chips and we ran it on the device.

         2. Dry Chip Sensitivity
In the previous experiment, using Ftaq batch D0411 at a 1:5 dilution, we increased the Ftaq concentration (plus trehalose and BSA) by 20% and 40% from the normal formulation, which improved Ftaq sensitivity at 66 copies/channel.
We then repeated the experiment using batch D1212 at a 1:40 dilution (since the Ftaq concentration in this batch is higher than that of batch D0411); however, the results appeared inconsistent.
Before proceeding further with experiments to improve Ftaq sensitivity, in my opinion it would be better to develop a quantitative or semi-quantitative polymerase calculation method. This would facilitate future enzyme development, both in liquid and dry formats. If the enzyme source has a consistent concentration, the results of other research activities will also be more consistent. Ode has experience in semi-quantitative protein quantification from his research prior to joining Formulatrix, and he may be able to try applying it again (just a small suggestion from my side).
c) Scale-up of dry chip production
On January 8, 2026, we will evaluate a production scenario from Munis by increasing output from 18 chips/day to 36 chips/day, and assess it using a systematic interval sampling method to determine whether this scale-up impacts dry chip quality.

            1. Demonstrating the specificity risk of allowing an fTaq reaction that was preheated to 85C to slowly cool to 60C before PCR cycling. (FUSA Testing).
Preheating the reaction to 85C prior to fTaq addition, then allowing the reaction to slowly cool to 60C (similar to current tongue swab device preheat sequence) results in high levels of non-specific primer-dimer amplification and no target-specific amplification -l ink. On the tongue swab device, the preheated fTaq reaction should not be allowed to cool below 85C prior to PCR cycling[h][i][j][k][l][m]. 


            2. Preliminary check on preheating to 85C during mixing. 
Previously, we have started to use 65C during preheating. When the temperature was raised to 85C and tested on dried chips, it flatlined. The chips from the same batch performed fine when the preheating temperature was set to 65C. Link to journal entry here Device Testing - H1 2026.  




Preheating set at 65 C
	  

	Preheating set at 85 C
	  

	



Since this was done with dried chips, there was a slight doubt on whether the dried assay in the channel is somehow affected negatively when the heatpad is ramping up its temperature. The dried assay is not directly on top of the heatpad, but 85C is relatively high and the dried assay is not too far from the heatpad. To proof this, the runs were repeated with liquid reactions with the preheating set to 65C and 85C. Turns out, a similar pattern was observed, where the reactions that had a higher preheating temperature performed very poorly.  Ignore the difference in reactions per chip for the dried test shown above to the liquid test shown below. The dried test used leftover chips that were made for dilution tests for a previous batch. 


Preheating set at 65 C
	  

	Preheating set at 85 C
	  

	



This leads me to believe that the higher temperature somehow negatively affects the components in the mixture.This results are also different from what was observed in FUSA, where preheating (up to 90C) improves specificity (judged by meltcurves). It is worth noting that there are a few differences between FUSA’s experiments and ours : 
            * fTaq is added after the preheating in FUSA. Here, the mix already contains all components since we cannot add anything after the chip is sealed.
            * FUSA uses a qPCR machine instead of TS device. 
            * Most of the data uses commercial Taq. 


Technically, all the components that we use (probes and primers, buffer, and  dNTPs, even the fTaq) are thermostable at 85C, or even higher. I am not sure how the combination of using our TS device (instead of a regular qPCR machine) and preheating for a brief moment causes the performance to become significantly worse. More tests needs to be done to understand what we can improve if we want to incorporate a preheating step. 


            * Is it the fTaq that is negatively affected by the heating that causes weak activity? Unlikely since taq is arguably more thermostable compared to all the other components. 
            * Other components somehow cannot handle the heat? If so, why? They’re all fine at 95C during the denaturation step. The preheating step does not take too long either. 
            * Is the liquid heating up too rapidly in the channel during the mixing step?
            * If the issue is heat-caused damage, it’s worth trying to add more sugar based enhancers. Most of them are known to act as stabilizers and thermoprotectants. We already use trehalose. Sucrose is another option.


            3. Adding Evagreen to TS tests.
Link to report Device Testing - H1 2026
The initial idea to add evagreen is to gauge the amount of nonspecific amplicons that’s produced when reactions are run in the TS device, especially when we use low copy gblocks. I ran some tests with and without fluorescence, with and without evagreen to see how it affected the baseline. 


In the buffer only runs, addition of evagreen did not increase the baseline significantly. However, when it is added to reactions that contains assays, the baseline increases. And the curve amplitude increases significantly in just the first few cycles of the log phase. This could indicate that there’s a lot of non-specific amplification happening and the evagreen is binding to all the double stranded nucleotides. It is also important to note that the oversaturation is caused by the relatively low limit of our lenses. It would be interesting to see if other polymerase other than fTaq (especially hotstart ones) has the same reactions

               * Baking Temperature Development
Last week, we evaluated a new chip baking parameter of 80 °C for 30 minutes to improve dry chip production yield. Under this condition, the Ct values, curve quality, and dried appearance were similar, with no significant differences observed. This week, we performed further testing at lower gBlock concentrations and evaluated the dryness level. The results indicate that both Ftaq and DS Bio HS exhibit good dryness levels. However, at lower gBlock copy numbers, Ftaq shows greater variability in Ct values and curve quality, which is most likely attributable to a sensitivity issue.                4. DMSO test on QS6 Machine for DSBio Regular and fTaq
Based on our experiment, addition of DMSO reduces the amplification curve quality, both for DSBio Regular and fTaq. It also reduces the melt curves.


  

	  

	

               5. Dilution test fTaq batch d1212 (in QS6)
We did a dilution test from undiluted to 1:60. fTaq d1212 has the best performance when it is diluted to 1:50. It is different compared to last fTaq’s batch when the best performance in liquid form is on dilution 1:10-1:20. We assume the differences from its results because the concentration of this fTaq production batch is higher than previous batches. First experiment, second experiment.


               6. Dried fTaq batch d1212 Performance in TS device. 
This experiment tested dried fTaq on the TS device, with the fTaq diluted from 1/20x to 1/50x. The previous batch of fTaq batch d0411 (diluted 1/5x) was used as a control to evaluate the differences between the two batches. From the runs, it shows that batch d1212 has the best performance when diluted to 1/30 - 1/40x, and had comparable performance when diluted even lower to a 1/5x fTaq d0411. In higher concentrations, it has poor performance. 


Other than different concentrations (final yield) between the two batches and the source culture used, there is no significant difference in the process of purifying these two batches of fTaq. But from the preliminary results, the two batches are observed to have different performances, with the d1212 being the better out of the two. 






               7. Cross Contamination Test
A preliminary test for BSL 2 testing using IS6110/IS1081, RIF and INH assays with several gblocks, in order to get cross reaction data when we use full process setup within the device. 


The result of this experiment is that the KatG and FabG targets were not amplified, which was attributed to the use of incorrectly selected gBlocks. Based on our prior data, WT gBlocks should be used rather than KatG and FabG gBlocks. 


Hereafter, IS6110 and IS1081 gBlocks were unexpectedly amplified by the RpoB (RIF) assay, despite not being the intended targets. This was evidenced by amplification observed in Test 1 and Test 2 when IS6110/IS1081 gBlocks were tested with the FS1/RS1 primer set, which is specific for the RIF assay. In addition, weak late-cycle amplification (approximately cycle 40) was detected in the IS6110/IS1081 channels in both tests. Here

               * Baselining DSbio reguler with IS1081/IS6110 and Human assay using HS DSbio buffer , also compare with HS DSbio and Ftaq. The result shows DSbio reguler and Ftaq has inconsistent result.

               * Baselining LOD HS DSbio with dried form assay and run with F2 dried chip using M1N2 buffer. the LOD with dried assay could detect at 50 cp/600 μL, which is equivalent to 3.33 cp/40 μL. However, this test was only run once for each concentration and still requires additional data.






  

               10. LED and Cap Heater Test
Continuation test from config 6 that successfully killed the MSM bacteria in both liquid and agar plate for 6 days. So, dika & altius optimized config 6 in order to shorten the heating process and we did both culture and PCR test as usual afterwards (all the vials were inverted in this test). Overall, the results shows:
               * By cq wise, all new configs had no significant difference compared to the default config (140 th, 5 mins beng beng).
               * On the agar plate result, all cap heater configs had no MSM bacteria growth after 3 and 6 days observation. In other words, a cap heater is effective in killing MSM bacteria.
               * In the liquid culture, only config 8 showed clear results after 3 and 6 days observation.

                           3. fTaq Sensitivity Improvement for Low Copy templates w/ DMSO
When using DMSO for almost all of my previous experiments, the amount used was always 2% for all template concentration. This amount was based on this testing, where the addition 2% DMSO had the most promising result when compared to 1-3%. However, I feel like that should have been explored more as the addition of 2% DMSO in  the upcoming tests did not help much with lower copies (660 and below). 


Another test was done with gradient levels of DMSO ranging from 2, 4 and 6% with 660 copies of templates (since this seems to be the cutoff for sensitivity). The experiment was repeated with annealing set at 55C and 57.5C. It appears that 4% DMSO increases PCR performance better as opposed to the original 2%, with the annealing set at 57.5 C for 660 copies of template. Between the two chosen annealing temperature, it is not quite clear which one is better, or if I can still tinker around with it (increasing it by one degree perhaps). The use of 57.5C is slightly better with the current amount of tests done when compared to 55C, but it has greater run-to-run variability. Here’s a comparison between the 2 temperatures with varying amount of DMSO with 660 copies of template (IS6110 FAM channel only). 
When the same set-up is tried for even lower copies (66 copies), similar enhancement was not observed. This can be explained by the fact that out fTaq has reached its limit, or there are some more alterations that can be done. The curve quality that is observed with 66copies is mostly flat, with some curves. Link to report : Device Testing - H1 2026  


Prior to this, I tried to combine adding  2%DMSO and additional fTaq to see if that would increase performance, since the 2%DMSO only would not help much when we are processing TS samples. The results did not show any significant improvement. Link to report : Direct PCR - H2 2025. 
I also did baseline testing for fTaq + DMSO on QS6, it  can be found here DE Lab Journal - H2 2025  


For the following week, I will be focusing on trying to reproduce the results with dried fTaq using formula 2, where I will do a LoD test for dried fTaq, and then attempt to see if DMSO in the resuspension buffer could increase sensitivity as it did with the liquid tests. 




                           4. Dry Chip Development 
Rif assay
In batch 630, channel 1 containing Meridian RIF appeared not sufficiently dried. I confirmed this with Adit and Bowo, and Bowo also observed that the same channel was not fully dry in several chips. For that chip, I only added the assay and Meridian. Normally, when using a single-plex with Meridian, I include NFW, but in that experiment I did not. I then repeated the process in batch 631. After adding NFW,the dried looks dry and the amplification curves look good. All three RIF variants I tested (Meridian Fs1/Rs1-FAM, Meridian Fs1/Rs1a-FAM, Meridian Fs1/Rs2a-FAM) showed good amplification.  In channel 4, the Meridian IS6110-FAM curve appears shorter in quality, and the slug also looks shorter than the others, even though its liquid volume was the same as the rest.I still don’t know the cause.


  





Sensitivity improvement
I understand that increasing the amount of enzyme means increasing the amount of preservative reagent as well. However, in this experiment, I only increased the amount of enzyme to determine the limit of the preservative reagent’s ability to maintain the enzyme. In this experiment, we can see that adding 20% more enzyme without increasing the preservative reagent improves the curve quality. However, after increasing the enzyme by 40% and then 60%, the curve quality drops significantly. This occurs because the amount of preservative reagent is no longer sufficient.  


If we want to increase the amount of polymerase, we also need to increase the preservative reagents, The preservative reagent must be kept at the concentration :  trehalose 1 M at 9.5% of the total dried volume, and BSA 10 mg/mL at 10.8% of the total dried volume. In the next experiment, I will increase the concentration of the preservative reagent in FTAq by adding 40% and 60% concentration increments.and then test it at a lower gBlock concentration.

Dr Tammy asks how we determine the gold standard, we answer the standard is to match with genexpert’s performance, and if available with TB culture test. She suggests we use a better device than genexpert, since genexpert only detects RIF. She introduces Genoscholar, which detects both RIF and INH. Disadvantage of Genoscholar is that the extraction and PCR process is separate. It only detects the amplification of each target and translates it to a strip band.

03/10/2026
Design review for USB-C power delivery, ultrasonic inverter board, and ultrasonic matching impedance board.
The design review is already done, the PCBs are placed to order.
  

Sonicator Driver: Increase transformer ratio.
The purpose of increasing the transformer in this test is to know the effect on sonication and what happens if the ratio is increased . In this experiment, we did a modification of the existing transformer by removing the secondary winding and recreating it with higher turns. The existing transformer currently used for our driver has primary winding 25 for each to center tap, and secondary winding 60 turns. For the new winding we create 90 turns so the transformer ratio changes from 1:2.5 to 1:3.6.
  
  
  

Fig 1. Rewinding process. Primary winding. New Secondary winding.
________________


We can calculate the number of turns by using:
,
The inductance factor (Al) for the transformer is 6.2uH.
Result:
After the transformer finishes assembly, we test it first by using a signal generator to see the voltage amplification on each frequency and the result is good, starting from 20KHz until 50KHz the voltage amplification is flat 3.6x.
Then test it with the driver using an old matching impedance network (L matching). The result is not good, when the driver is set to resonance frequency, the transformer produces a ringing sound and the waveform is not stable.
Video tests.
It also found that the current draw from the power supply increased drastically until the power supply changed to constant current and the voltage drop.
    Fig 2. 1:3.6 transformer test on the driver.
The cavitation still occurs but not optimum caused by an unstable voltage source because of the power supply switch to constant current mode.
Analysis:
For the transformer, if there is a load on the connected secondary side, the primary side will not see the same impedance value. The primary side sees the load through the lens of the transformer turns ratio.
If  is the ratio of primary secondary transformer:
, then the impedance seen by the primary side is:

For 2.5 ratio,
,
For 3.6 ration,

For the 2.5 ratio, the current drawn from the power supply is 4.5A the impedance seen by the primary is approximately 2.67 Ohm mean while on the secondary side the impedance is 16.68 Ohm. When the transformer was changed to 1:3.6, the impedance seen by the primary side now drop to 0.771 * 16.68 = 1.28Ohm. With the same matching configuration, the power supply should provide around 9.37A current to handle this load. Because the existing power supply can only provide 5.7A max, that's why the power supply drops the voltage and enters constant current mode.
In the other things, maybe the imperfect winding process also affects the transformer performance. 


To answer the curiosity, then we tried to use the handheld transformer that has 1:4 ratio but with better wire winding and it also uses a litz wire.
  

Fig 3. Use handheld ultrasonic transformer drivers with 1:4 turns ratio.


Results:
The power supply still can enter constant current, but with careful tuning of the circuit, the waveform can be a little bit stable and can produce good sonication.
  

  

Fig 4. Testing 1:4 transformer from handheld driver
It also has been tested with the temperature increase. Even though the power supply keeps dropping, increasing the ratio can increase the temperature Ramp Up if compared with 1:2.5 ratio. Even the difference is not so much, but this is a sign that the transformer ratio can help to increase the force or velocity of transducer vibration the same as if we increase the power supply voltage.
  

Fig 5. Temperature increases in comparison.
For 1:2.5 ratio, temperature ramp up is 1.58C/s, max temperature for 25 cycles is 67.3C.
For 1:4 ratio, temperature ramp up is 1.65C/s, maximum temperature for 25 cycles is 70.3C.
For the excitation voltage, since the transformer ratio was increased, the excitation voltage should be increased. But because at this test the power supply enters constant current and the voltage drops, so the excitation voltage is a little bit the same with the 1:2.5 ratio.


One more thing we got from this test is that the impedance on the secondary side when the driver is set close to resonance is quite small, 16.8 Ohms. But the question is, is 16.8 Ohm the real impedance or dominated by the imaginary part ?
Because we are missing the phase shift data between voltage and current, so are not yet can be found in which area this impedance belongs to ?


To analyze the phase margin, we plan to use zero crossing to understand the phase margin between current and voltage.
 In a Resonant Converter design (like the one you created), we are not looking for a 0 phase between the voltage peak and the current peak, but rather ZVS (Zero Current Switching) or ZCS (Zero Current Switching). 
ZCS (Zero Current Switching): we must ensure that the zero current point (when the current changes from negative to positive) occurs exactly at the voltage transition from the bottom box to the top box. If the current reaches zero before the voltage transition, our system is capacitive (leading). If the current reaches zero after the transition, our system is inductive (lagging).

Experiment with alternative matching impedance network for reliable sonicator driver.
While supporting seno to debug the device-006, we also did some experiment on the sonicator driver by modifying the matching impedance network that might help to improve the sonication reliability.
Current impedance matching network for the driver is called L matching, which consists of a series inductor and parallel with the compensation capacitor. It only has one voltage magnification at the cutoff frequency (the highest Q), below or higher than the resonant and cutoff frequency, the impedance will shift (dominated by inductive or capacitive).
  
  

Fig 1. Current L matching impedance network.
The L match is the simplest matching network with enough sonication performance. But this matching network only has 1 center frequency and the bandwidth is narrow. To remember the L matching performance, here is the temperature increase and ramp-up at the testing:
Ramp-up 1.56C/s.
  

Fig 2. L Matching impedance network temperature increase.
________________


To find another better impedance matching, we already did some experiments to add another matching network together with the L matching. The alternative matching networks are called:
1. LCCL matching. Consists of L series, C parallel, C series, and L parallel. The purpose of adding C series and L parallel is to create second resonant and cut-off frequencies to have wider bandwidth.
2. LCCCL matching. Consists of L series paralleled with C (LC series), C parallel, C series, and L parallel. Adding paralleled C on the L series is to create anti resonance after higher cutoff frequency to remove third harmonic and later that may still exist.
Detailed results can be seen here.
  

LCCL.
Compared to L matching, this network still has lower performance based on cavitation and temperature increase even though the current is almost the same with L matching. Video.
  
  

Fig 3. LCCL matching impedance and its response (simulation)


  
  

Fig 4. LCCL cavitation and temperature ramp-up compared to L matching
LCCCL.
Compared to L matching, this network has a little bit higher cavitation performance and temperature ramp-up. 
  
  

Fig 5. LCCCL network and its response (simulation)


  

Fig 6. LCCCL cavitation and temperature ramp-up compared to L matching and LCCL

TPS2492 Hot-Swap Controller for 20V Type-C Powered System with Inrush Current Limiting 
Measured system behavior shows:
* Peak inrush current: 27.7 A
* Initial spike duration: 10 µs
* Maximum inrush window: up to 500 µs
* Input supply: 20V Type-C adapter with overcurrent protection at 5A
To ensure reliable startup without triggering adapter protection, a hot-swap controller was implemented using the Texas Instruments TPS2492. This device enables controlled inrush current limiting, programmable fault timeout, and safe MOSFET ramp-up for capacitive loads and switching regulator inputs.
Current Limiter
The TPS2492 monitors the voltage drop across an external current sense resistor connected between the source of the MOSFET and the load return path. When the voltage across RSENSE reaches the internal threshold:
VSENSE(th) = 50 mV (typical)
the controller enters current regulation mode.In this mode:
* The IC reduces MOSFET gate drive
* The MOSFET operates in linear region
* Load current is clamped at a constant value
* Inrush current is actively limited
Adapter Type C (Max.5A), ILimit  target (safe) = 4.5 A so Vsense = 45 mV
ILimit = Vsense / RSense
4.5A = 45 mV / RSense
RSense = 0.01 Ω 
RSENSE (mΩ)
	ILIMIT (Approx.)
	Risk to 5A Adapter (OCP)
	Engineering Note
	5 mΩ
	9–10 A
	Adapter will definitely trip
	Excessive inrush, not suitable for Type-C current-limited supply
	8 mΩ
	6 A
	High risk of OCP triggering
	May still exceed adapter protection threshold
	10 mΩ
	4.5–5 A
	Safest / most balanced choice
	Good balance between fast startup and adapter protection margin
	15 mΩ
	3 A
	Very safe for adapter
	Startup ramp becomes slower due to stronger current limiting
	

Fault Timeout (C Timer)
The CTIMER pin defines the fault timeout duration. This means: How long the IC allows an overcurrent or current-limiting condition before it is considered a fault and the device shuts down. CTIMER mainly determines:
* The allowed time during input capacitor charging (inrush phase).

* The tolerance time before shutdown if the current is still in the current limit.
Ctotal on the SAMBo is about 1036 UF (Motor Drivers, buck converters, Hotswap output).   








 1.3 to 2   → Min.safe 6 ms
Formula (on the datasheet):
VTH = 1.23V
ITIMER= 50 µA




 (Nearest standard value→ 220nF or 270nF).


02/10/2026
Debug SAMBo v3 USB-C power triggered over current protection.
During test v3 system 1, we found that using a USB-C power adapter when the power push button pushed, the adapter suddenly tripped and showed an over current indicator on the LED. This issue happens on all of the USB-C power adapters, but not on the power bank battery. We check there is no short circuit, and the load without the motor should be less than 1A. We suspect that there is a high inrush current on the board, based on the last experience we had a few half ago, the inrush current was caused by many bulk capacitors on the board. From the measurement using a current probe, when the power button is pressed, the current increases to 27.7A @10uS and keeps high up to 500uS. This duration (500uS) is enough for the USB-C power adapter to detect the over current and trigger the protection.
  
  

Fig 1. Power test
The root cause based on the last same issue, is there are big capacitors in the load side, and it causes a high current draw during initial capacitor charge. 
  
  

Fig 2. Bulk capacitor on the motor driver
The bulk capacitor has a purpose to reduce the dI/dt when the motor energizes by storing some energy in the capacitor and it will provide the current for a short period of time and will not draw high current directly to the adapter. But, on the other hand it will cause high inrush current and if it is seen by the adapter it will trigger the over current protection in the power adapter.
To mitigate this problem, first we remove the 1x 330uF capacitors, remove 2x 300uS:
The removed 1x 330uF capacitor still has the same inrush current in the same duration and still causes a power trip on the USB-C adapter. Removed 2x 330uF capacitors reduce the inrush current at the same duration and don’t cause power trip.
  
  

Fig 3. Left: removed 1x 330uF capacitor, Right: removed 2x 330uF capacitors
________________


Here is the inrush current difference before removing the capacitor, remove 1x 330uF capacitor, remove 2x 330uF capacitor.


  

  

  

Fig 4. Top: 2x 330uF exists, mid: removed 1x 330uF, bottom: removed 2x 330uF capacitors




The 330uF is needed to maintain dI/dt keep lower during motor driver draw current, if we remove this capacitor, it might create another issue regarding higher dI/dT. Because the issue is power trip on the adapter, we need to decrease the high inrush current when the power button is pressed. It means that, before supplying current to the main board, it should provide enough energy for some short period to minimize direct current draw from the power adapter. The power distribution is divided into 3 stages:
  

The USB-C PD board will request the voltage and max current to the power adapter, if requested power is available, the power adapter will give the output. From this diagram, the USB-C power delivery board should provide enough current for a short period of time by placing some bulk capacitor to avoid direct high current draw to the power adapter, so the dI/dT and the inrush current can be minimized. After take a look on the power delivery board schematic, there is only 1 22uF (C1) capacitor on the MOSFET output:
  
 
Because on the mainboard power controller, the bulk capacitor is placed after P-Channel mosfet, before button pressed the bulk capacitor charge is still empty, after button pressed the power module(USB-C PD) should supply an amount of current to charge the bulk capacitor on the mainboard. Because there is only 1 capacitor (22uF) on the USB-C PD, it is not enough to provide the needed charge from the mainboard so the USB-C module will draw a lot of current from the power adapter to provide the needed charge for the mainboard for a short period of time. This timeline will generate high inrush current and then be detected by the power adapter and trigger the power adapter protection. By this, we can put a bigger capacitor on the USB-C to provide an amount of charge. If the mainboard requests a short period for charging its bulk capacitor, the power module (USB-C) still has enough charge without drawing large current from the power adapter.
After adding 1x 330uF on the USB-C power delivery out, now the mainboard can turn on normally and the inrush current is not detected by the current probe and also the power adapter didn’t trigger the over current protection.
  


Fig 5. Add 1 bulk capacitor and test to the system
Another advanced solution is using efuse, and we already test using TPS16530PWPR. It can reduce the inrush current much and also have an over current protection and over temperature protection. Using the efuse might also eliminate traditional fuse.

Debug Sonicator Driver.
In this test we are still curious why the feedback signal from the current transformer doesn't have the same phase to the excitation voltage even the frequency and matching network close to the transducer resonance frequency. 
If we take a look at the method to measure the resonance frequency using the impedance bridge, when the frequency is matched with the transducer frequency, the output signal will be the same phase from signal input.
     
But if we compare the excitation voltage with the current sensed by the current transformer, it seems that the phase margin stays the same even if it was resonant or out of resonance. Only current amplitude will change if the frequency is resonant or not. 
  
  

Fig 1. Left: Out of resonance, right: in resonance.
We suspect that, maybe it is because of a transformer present on the driver, so the leakage inductance and the parasitic capacitance cause this. If so, the phase still should be shifted if the frequency shifted or changed. To find the clue, we tried to measure the current from the power supply not on the transducer. We also removed the current feedback on the driver and manually tuning the potentiometer to find the correct frequency.
  

The result is, the phase shift between excitation voltage and the current is the same with the feedback to excitation.
  
  

  

Fig 2. Left: frequency far from resonance (incorrect matching net). Right: frequency near with the resonance frequency (almost correct matching net)
From this result, we know that the comparison between excitation voltage and the current (from transducer or power supply) have the same phase margin along the frequency. If we take a look again on the impedance bridge, the voltage signal is the source signal from the signal generator. But in our case, the excitation signal is converted from square wave into sine wave by accumulating energy on the inductance and capacitance + transducer and creating LC series resonance. And thus sine wave is not the source voltage signal if we want to compare with the current. The source signal itself is the square wave from the generator. So from this we should compare the current waveform with the square wave as the voltage to know the phase margin in every frequency.
Then we tried to measure again, and observe the phase margin between square wave signal and current waveform from the current transformer.
  
  

Fig 3. Left: On resonance frequency, Right: off resonance frequency
If we compare the current waveform to square wave, there is a phase shift on the several frequencies. But to determine the phase shift, because it is a different waveform, we need to know how to measure the phase shift between the square wave and the sine wave. Or if we can modulate the square wave in tho sine wave without distorting the phase it might be easy to measure the phase shift.
